name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
كَلَّا وَٱلْقَمَرِ (٣٢)
```

```english
No indeed! By the Moon! (32)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذْ أَدْبَرَ (٣٣)
```

```english
By the night when it recedes! (33)
```

```arabic
وَٱلصُّبْحِ إِذَآ أَسْفَرَ (٣٤)
```

```english
By the dawn when it brightens! (34)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهَا لَإِحْدَى ٱلْكُبَرِ (٣٥)
```

```english
Indeed they are one of the greatest [signs of God] (35)
```

```arabic
نَذِيرًا لِّلْبَشَرِ (٣٦)
```

```english
—a warner to all humans, (36)
```

```arabic
لِمَن شَآءَ مِنكُمْ أَن يَتَقَدَّمَ أَوْ يَتَأَخَّرَ (٣٧)
```

```english
[alike] for those of you who like to advance ahead and those who would remain behind. (37)
```

```arabic
كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ (٣٨)
```

```english
Every soul is hostage to what it has earned, (38)
```

```arabic
إِلَّآ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٣٩)
```

```english
except the People of the Right Hand. (39)
```

```arabic
فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (٤٠)
```

```english
[They will be] in gardens, questioning (40)
```

```arabic
عَنِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٤١)
```

```english
the guilty: (41)
```

```arabic
مَا سَلَكَكُمْ فِى سَقَرَ (٤٢)
```

```english
‘What drew you into Hell?’ (42)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ لَمْ نَكُ مِنَ ٱلْمُصَلِّينَ (٤٣)
```

```english
They will answer, ‘We were not among those who prayed. (43)
```

```arabic
وَلَمْ نَكُ نُطْعِمُ ٱلْمِسْكِينَ (٤٤)
```

```english
Nor did we feed the poor. (44)
```

```arabic
وَكُنَّا نَخُوضُ مَعَ ٱلْخَآئِضِينَ (٤٥)
```

```english
We used to indulge in [profane] gossip along with the gossipers, (45)
```

```arabic
وَكُنَّا نُكَذِّبُ بِيَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (٤٦)
```

```english
and we used to deny the Day of Retribution (46)
```

```arabic
حَتَّىٰٓ أَتَىٰنَا ٱلْيَقِينُ (٤٧)
```

```english
until death came to us.’ (47)
```

```arabic
فَمَا تَنفَعُهُمْ شَفَـٰعَةُ ٱلشَّـٰفِعِينَ (٤٨)
```

```english
So the intercession of the intercessors will not avail them. (48)
```

## (Commentary)

In these verses, the Holy Quran is exonerated from the accusations they cast upon it. It is established as one of the greatest divine signs (الآيات الإلهية الكبرى), containing a warning (إنذار) to all humanity. Furthermore, following it leads to the liberation of souls from the mortgage (رهانة) of their deeds, which otherwise drive them toward Hell (سقر).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَلَّا** — **No indeed!** (74:32) is a rebuff (ردع) and a rejection of what preceded. It is mentioned in *al-Kashshaf*: "It is a rejection—after having made it a reminder—that it should ever be a reminder for them, because they do not take heed; or it is a rebuff to those who deny that 'one of the greatest' is a warner." End quote. According to the first view, it is a rejection of what preceded, and according to the second, it is a rebuff of what is to come. There is another perspective that will follow.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالْقَمَرِ وَاللَّيْلِ إِذْ أَدْبَرَ وَالصُّبْحِ إِذا أَسْفَرَ** — **By the moon, and by the night when it withdraws, and by the dawn when it brightens,** (74:32-34) consists of oath after oath. The withdrawal (إدبار) of the night is the opposite of its approach, and the brightening (إسفار) of the dawn is its manifestation and disclosure.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّها لَإِحْدَى الْكُبَرِ** — **it is indeed one of the greatest [portents],** (74:35). Commentators have mentioned that the pronoun refers to Hell (سقر), and "the greatest" (الكبر) is the plural of "the greatest [feminine]" (كبرى).

The meaning of Hell (سقر) being "one of the greatest" is that it is one of the greatest calamities (الدواهي الكبر) to which no other calamity can be compared, just as it is said: "He is one of the men," meaning he has no peer among them. This sentence serves as the response to the oath (جواب القسم).

The meaning is: I swear by such and such that Hell (سقر) is indeed one of the greatest calamities—the greatest of them—as a warning to humanity.

It is not improbable that **كَلَّا** (No indeed!) is a rebuff to his saying regarding the Quran: **إِنْ هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ يُؤْثَرُ إِنْ هذا إِلَّا قَوْلُ الْبَشَرِ** — **‘This is nothing but magic handed down. This is nothing but the word of man.’** (74:24-25). In this case, the pronoun in **إِنَّها** (it is indeed) would refer to the Quran, insofar as it consists of signs (آيات), or it may be a case of the noun of *inna* (اسم إن) matching its predicate (خبر).

The meaning would then be: It is not as he said; I swear by such and such that the Quran—its signs—is indeed one of the greatest divine signs (الآيات الإلهية الكبرى) as a warning to humanity.

It has also been said that the sentence **إِنَّها لَإِحْدَى الْكُبَرِ** (it is indeed one of the greatest) is a justification (تعليل) for the rebuff, and the oath is parenthetical for emphasis, having no response, or its response is implicit, indicated by **كَلَّا** (No indeed!).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **نَذِيراً لِلْبَشَرِ** — **a warner to humans,** (74:36). This is a verbal noun (مصدر) meaning "warning" (الإنذار), functioning as a specification (تمييز). It has also been said to be a circumstantial qualifier (حال) derived from the context of His saying: **إِنَّها لَإِحْدَى الْكُبَرِ** (it is indeed one of the greatest), meaning: it has become great and momentous while being a warning (نذيرا), i.e., warning (منذرة).

Other views have been mentioned which are not worthy of consideration, such as the claim of some that it is an attribute of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and that the verse is connected to the beginning of the Surah, the implied meaning being: "Arise as a warner to humanity and warn." Others claimed it is an attribute of Allah, the Exalted.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لِمَنْ شاءَ مِنْكُمْ أَنْ يَتَقَدَّمَ أَوْ يَتَأَخَّرَ** — **for such of you as may wish to go forward or stay behind.** (74:37). This is a generalization of the warning. The phrase **لِمَنْ شاءَ** (for such as may wish) is a substitute (بدل) for "humans" (البشر), and **أَنْ يَتَقَدَّمَ** (to go forward) etc., is the object of "wish" (شاء). What is meant by "going forward" and "staying behind" is following the truth (الاتباع للحق)—the manifestation of which is faith (الإيمان) and obedience (الطاعة)—and not following it—the manifestation of which is disbelief (الكفر) and disobedience (المعصية).

The meaning is: A warner for those among you who follow the truth and for those who do not; that is, for all of you without exception.

It has also been said that **أَنْ يَتَقَدَّمَ** (to go forward) is in the nominative case as a subject (مبتدأ) and **لِمَنْ شاءَ** (for such as may wish) is its predicate (خبر), similar to saying: "For whoever has performed ablution is to pray." The meaning would be: Absolutely, for whoever wishes to go forward or stay behind, let him go forward or stay behind. This is like His saying: **فَمَنْ شاءَ فَلْيُؤْمِنْ وَمَنْ شاءَ فَلْيُكْفُرْ** — **‘So let anyone who wishes believe, and let anyone who wishes disbelieve.’** (18:29). The meaning of going forward and staying behind is preceding toward good and lagging behind it. End quote.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ** — **Every soul is a hostage to what it has earned,** (74:38). The preposition *bi* (بـ) means "with," or denotes causality (السببية), or exchange (المقابلة). The word **رَهِينَةٌ** (hostage) means a pledge (الرهن), as mentioned by al-Zamakhshari. He said in *al-Kashshaf*: "**رَهِينَةٌ** is not the feminine form of **رَهِينٌ** (hostage) found in His saying: **كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِما كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ** — **‘Every man is a hostage to what he has earned.’** (52:21) for the sake of the femininity of the soul (النفس). This is because if that were intended, it would have been said **رَهِينٌ**, since the form *fa‘īl* (فعيل) in the sense of *maf‘ūl* (مفعول) is the same for both masculine and feminine. Rather, it is a noun meaning 'pledge' (الرهن), like **الشتيمة** (insult) meaning **الشتم** (insulting). It is as if it were said: 'Every soul is a pledge (رهن) for what it has earned.'" End quote.

The subtle point in considering every soul a hostage (رهينة) is that Allah has over it the right of servitude (العبادة) through faith and righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة). Thus, it is a hostage, preserved and detained with Allah until it pays its debt and fulfills the right of the Exalted. If it believes and acts righteously, it is ransomed and released; but if it disbelieves, commits crimes, and dies in that state, it remains a permanently detained hostage. This is distinct from it being a hostage (رهين) to its deeds—inseparable from what it has earned of good or evil—as previously mentioned in His saying, the Exalted: **كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِما كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ** — **‘Every man is a hostage to what he has earned.’** (52:21).

The verse is in the position of explaining the reason for the generalization derived from His saying: **نَذِيراً لِلْبَشَرِ لِمَنْ شاءَ مِنْكُمْ أَنْ يَتَقَدَّمَ أَوْ يَتَأَخَّرَ** (a warner to humans, for such of you as may wish to go forward or stay behind). For the fact that the human soul is a hostage to what it has earned necessitates that every soul should be wary of the Fire in which it will be imprisoned if it commits crimes and does not follow the truth.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِلَّا أَصْحابَ الْيَمِينِ** — **except the People of the Right Hand.** (74:39). These are those who are given their records in their right hands on the Day of Reckoning. They are the people of true beliefs and righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) among the average believers. Their mention and their designation as "the People of the Right Hand" (أصحاب اليمين) recur in several places in His speech, the Exalted. Accordingly, the exception is connected (متصل).

What is gathered from the combination of the excepted and the exception is the division of souls that possess "earning" (كسب) into: souls that are hostages to what they have earned—which are the souls of the criminals (المجرمين)—and souls that are ransomed from the pledge and released—which are the souls of the People of the Right Hand. As for the Foremost (السابقون), the Near Ones (المقربون)—those whom Allah mentioned in various places of His speech and counted as a third group, as in His saying, the Exalted: **وَكُنْتُمْ أَزْواجاً ثَلاثَةً** — **‘and you will be three groups:’** (56:7) until He says: **وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ** — **‘And the Foremost Ones are the foremost ones: they are the ones brought near [to Allah],’** (56:10-11)—these have settled in the station of servitude (العبادة) where they possess neither themselves nor the deeds of themselves. Their souls belong to Allah, as do their deeds; thus, they are neither summoned nor reckoned. The Exalted said: **فَإِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ** — **‘they will surely be hauled [into punishment]—[all] except the chosen servants of Allah.’** (37:127-128). Therefore, they are outside this classification entirely.

It has been narrated from some that the People of the Right Hand are the angels; from others, that they are the children of the Muslims; from others, that they are those who were on the right of Adam on the Day of the Covenant (يوم الميثاق); and from others, that they are those for whom the "goodly reward" (الحسنى) has preceded from Allah. These are weak views whose weakness is not hidden.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فِي جَنَّاتٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ عَنِ الْمُجْرِمِينَ ما سَلَكَكُمْ فِي سَقَرَ** — **[They will be] in gardens, questioning concerning the guilty: ‘What drew you into Hell?’** (74:40-42). **فِي جَنَّاتٍ** (In gardens) is the predicate of a deleted subject, and the nunation (تنوين) of "gardens" is for magnification (التعظيم). The implied meaning is: "They are in gardens whose description cannot be grasped." It is also possible for it to be a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the People of the Right Hand.

His saying: **يَتَساءَلُونَ عَنِ الْمُجْرِمِينَ** (questioning concerning the guilty) means their group asks about the group of the criminals.

His saying: **ما سَلَكَكُمْ فِي سَقَرَ** (What drew you into Hell?)—meaning what caused you to enter Hell—is an explanation of their questioning, either as an apposition of one sentence to another or by assuming the word "saying" (قول), i.e., saying: "What drew you into Hell?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا لَمْ نَكُ مِنَ الْمُصَلِّينَ** — **They will say, ‘We were not among those who prayed.** (74:43). The plural pronoun refers to the criminals (المجرمين). What is meant by prayer (الصلاة) is the specific devotional turning (التوجه العبادي) toward Allah, the Glorified; thus, it is not affected by the differences in the quantity or quality of prayer across different true heavenly religions.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَمْ نَكُ نُطْعِمُ الْمِسْكِينَ** — **Nor did we feed the poor.** (74:44). What is meant by feeding the poor (إطعام المسكين) is spending on the destitute of society to sustain them and fulfill their needs. Feeding the poor is an indication of the rights of people (حق الناس) in practice, just as prayer is an indication of the right of Allah (حق الله).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكُنَّا نَخُوضُ مَعَ الْخائِضِينَ** — **We used to gossip with the gossipers,** (74:45). What is meant by "gossiping" (الخوض) is engaging in falsehood (الباطل) in word or deed and delving deep into it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكُنَّا نُكَذِّبُ بِيَوْمِ الدِّينِ** — **and we used to deny the Day of Retribution,** (74:46), which is the Day of Recompense. These are four characteristics that a criminal is naturally prone to be afflicted with, either all or some of them. Since those answering the question are the collective group of criminals, it is correct to attribute all these traits to the whole group, even if some were afflicted with some and not others.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **حَتَّى أَتانَا الْيَقِينُ** — **until death came to us.’** (74:47). This is a limit for the denial. Commentators have interpreted "certainty" (اليقين) as death, because it is something about which there is no doubt. Thus, the meaning is: "We used to deny the Day of Recompense in the world until death came to us and worldly life was cut off," meaning: "We denied it as long as life lasted."

It has also been said that it refers to the certainty (اليقين) that arises regarding the truth of the Day of Recompense upon witnessing the signs of the Hereafter and beholding the intermediate realm (الحياة البرزخية) — *the state between death and resurrection* — at the time of death and after it. This is a fine meaning.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَما تَنْفَعُهُمْ شَفاعَةُ الشَّافِعِينَ** — **So the intercession of the intercessors will not avail them.** (74:48). It was previously mentioned in the discussion on intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — that this verse contains an indication that there are indeed intercessors who intercede and whose intercession is accepted, but their intercession does not benefit these people because they are deprived of attaining it.

We have cited a number of narrations (أخبار) regarding intercession in the first volume of this book.
